emitted.title: Force-unwrap on optional network response
emitted.body: The line `return response!` force-unwraps the result from `network.fetchUser(id:)`, which previously returned `User?`. If the network layer returns `nil` for a missing or invalid user, this will crash at runtime. The method signature change from `-> User?` to `-> User` suggests an API contract change, but the force-unwrap is unsafe.
emitted.suggested_action: Either keep the optional return type `-> User?` and return `response`, or throw a descriptive error when `response` is `nil` using `guard let user = response else { throw ... }`.

expected.rationale: response! crashes if network returns nil; should propagate as throws or return Optional

emitted.title: Force-unwrap on UserDefaults string value
emitted.body: The line `return UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: "username")!` force-unwraps the result of `string(forKey:)`, which returns `nil` if the key doesn't exist or the value is not a string. This will crash if "username" has never been set or was removed.
emitted.suggested_action: Return an optional `String?`, provide a default value with `?? ""`, or guard and throw an error if the username is required to exist.

expected.rationale: UserDefaults.string returns Optional<String>; force-unwrapping crashes on first launch when key is absent

emitted.title: Source-breaking return type change removes optionality
emitted.body: The return type changed from `User?` to `User`, removing optionality. This is a source-breaking change that will break all existing callers using optional binding (`if let user = try await fetchUser(...)`) or optional chaining (`try await fetchUser(...)?.name`). Callers compiled against the previous signature will fail to compile.
emitted.suggested_action: Restore the `User?` return type to maintain source compatibility, or version this as a new method (e.g., `fetchUserNonOptional`) and deprecate the old one.

expected.rationale: Public method changed from User? to User — source-breaking change for callers. Per steering, source-breaking signature changes are blocker.

emitted.title: Public method returns non-optional String for potentially absent value
emitted.body: The method returns `String` (non-optional) from `UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey:)`, which returns `String?`. This API design forces the implementation to crash when the key is absent rather than surfacing the optionality in the signature. Callers have no way to handle the missing-username case gracefully.
emitted.suggested_action: Change the return type to `String?` to accurately reflect that the username may not be present, allowing callers to handle the nil case.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() returns non-optional String but the underlying UserDefaults.string(forKey:) is Optional<String>. The signature lies to callers about whether nil is possible. Should be -> String? or take a default.

emitted.title: New public method `currentUserName()` has no test coverage
emitted.body: A new public method `currentUserName()` was added without any corresponding test in this diff. This method reads from UserDefaults and force-unwraps the result, which introduces potential runtime behavior that should be verified through tests.
emitted.suggested_action: Add a test that verifies `currentUserName()` returns the expected username when the UserDefaults key is set.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() is a new public method introduced in this diff with no accompanying test. Per test_coverage.md, new public/open declarations without a test entry point in the diff are flagged at severity major.

emitted.title: Retain cycle: missing `[weak self]` in Combine sink
emitted.body: The closure on line 17 captures `self` strongly (line 18: `self.handleEvent(event)`), creating a retain cycle. The `EventBus` instance holds `cancellables`, which stores this sink, which holds `self`. The subscription will never be released.
emitted.suggested_action: Change line 17 to `.sink { [weak self] event in` and safely unwrap `self` inside the closure.

expected.rationale: The .sink closure captures self strongly and is stored in cancellables, which is owned by self — a textbook Combine retain cycle. Per correctness.md blocker definition (`will crash, corrupt data, or violate a documented invariant`), a retain cycle leaks memory but does not crash; therefore severity is major, not blocker.
